All 12 gaps are now verified against ground truth. I have everything needed to emit the revised document.

# Threads of God (TOG) Remake — Technical Design Document

*Generated by `tdd-generator-hybrid` (Variant C). Grounded against the C++ scaffold at `/Users/mbschenk/ClaudeCode/references/TOG-Remake/Source` and the project's ground-truth design reference `docs/TOG-GAS-Architecture.md` (the Phase-0 inspection of the original UE 5.4 Blueprint game). Every architectural claim cites a file/type or that reference. The reference's design intent is labeled `[DESIGN]` and is distinguished throughout from what the C++ scaffold actually implements today. Unsettled items are tagged `[OPEN]`; inferred bridging is `[ASSUMED]`; proposed-not-stated targets are `[PROPOSED]`.*

---

## 1. One-liner / executive summary

TOG-Remake is a **UE 5.7 C++/GAS rebuild of *Threads of God*** (originally UE 5.4 pure-Blueprint). The project's stated north star is not a 1:1 port: *"We are not remaking TOG's architecture with GAS. We are making TOG — the game — with GAS architecture"* (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:5`). The original was a **component-per-feature** Blueprint game; the rebuild **collapses most per-feature components into one shared ability stack and expresses state as GameplayTags + Attributes**, keeping animation as the timing authority.

**The single most important structural finding** (verbatim from the reference): *the player and the boss share **one attack-execution core** — only the **trigger** differs (player = input/combo buffer; boss = Behavior Tree)* (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:19,23`). The reference grounds this not just in the conclusion but in a **two-actor disposition asymmetry**: the boss has **no QTE/Dodge/LockOn/Skill/Combo components** — those are player-only — which is the evidence that the *shared* core is specifically **attack execution + hit reaction + parry**, granted to both pawns, while everything else is per-pawn ability sets (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:25,52`; see §2). The GAS shape is therefore **one shared attack-ability family with two trigger front-ends**: EnhancedInput→input-tag (player) and a BT task→`TryActivateAbilityByTag` (boss), both funneling into the same montage→hitbox-sweep→damage-spine→reaction tail (§3.2–§3.3).

**The defining aesthetic the rebuild must reproduce** (the reference's #1-emphasized feel) is **cross-actor reaction PAIRING**: an attacker's animation drives a reaction montage on the *victim*, so the two combatants animate as **one synchronized exchange**, not two independent montages — the literal source of TOG's connected swordplay feel (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:21,134`). In GAS this becomes the attacker ability firing a *targeted gameplay event* at the victim's ASC, which activates the victim's reaction ability to play the *paired* montage, timed by the attacker (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:21,134`). The reference grounds this as GAS-idiomatic with a concrete Aura precedent (§3.6). **In the current scaffold this exchange is only half-built**: the spine fires a one-directional `Event.Reaction.HitReact` to the victim via `SendGameplayEventToActor` (`TOGAttributeSet.cpp:104`) — an **event-activation** route, not the Aura `TryActivateAbilitiesByTag` tag-activation route the reference cites as the model (§3.6); the attacker-side pairing slot and the paired-montage selection do not yet exist in C++.

**State of the codebase:** this is a **C++ scaffold (55 `.h`/`.cpp` files under `Source/`, counted by `find`), not yet a playable game** — written but **not yet compiled** as of 2026-05-30 (`ONBOARDING.md:72`; the file's own header line `ONBOARDING.md:35` rounds this to "~35 files," but the verified count from `find Source -name '*.h' -o -name '*.cpp'` is **55** — cite :35 only for the "written but not compiled" status, not for the file count). It implements the *numeric* combat spine and the seams into which the design's animation/data/UI systems plug; most design systems (paired parry/counter/finisher execution, the poise economy, enemy AI, the HUD, weapon-swap, dodge, skills, ranged/aim, save/load) exist on paper in the reference but **not yet in C++**. There are **no automated tests** in the source tree (verified: no `FAutomationTest`/`*.Spec.cpp`/test module/target).

## 2. Goals & non-goals

### Goals (each independently checkable)
- **Rebuild TOG's *feel* on idiomatic GAS**, not its Blueprint structure — variation comes from variations of abilities/effects/tags/cues, letting GAS idioms decide structure (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:5`).
- **One shared attack-execution core, two trigger front-ends** (player input/combo vs boss BT) — the central structural goal, evidenced by the §2-below disposition asymmetry (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:19,25,52`).
- **Reproduce cross-actor reaction pairing** (the defining aesthetic): attacker animation drives the victim's reaction montage as a synchronized exchange (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:21,134`).
- **Keep animation a first-class logic layer in two forms**: notify-states define timing *windows*, and anim *curves* drive locomotion + turn-in-place while a montage plays — without bespoke tick code (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:17`; §3.6a).
- **Two-module split** keeping data/contracts (`TOGCore`) separate from gameplay (`TOG`) — verifiable in `TOG.uproject` Modules and the two `*.Build.cs` files.
- **Single numeric damage pipeline** with separate **health** and **poise** channels through one ExecCalc and one AttributeSet hook (`ExecCalc_Damage.cpp`, `TOGAttributeSet.cpp::PostGameplayEffectExecute`).
- **Data-/animation-driven attack authoring**: abilities read montage+event+socket+cue from `FTOG_TaggedMontage`; combo chains from `FTOGComboRow`; damage windows fire from anim notifies (`TOGTypes.h`, `AN_MontageEvent.cpp`).
- **Replication-correct ASC placement**: player ASC on PlayerState (Mixed), enemy ASC on the pawn (Minimal) (`TOGPlayerState.cpp:8,10`, `TOGEnemy.cpp:10,12`).
- **Custom per-hit metadata surviving replication** via a derived effect context (`FTOGGameplayEffectContext` + `NetSerialize`).

### The two-actor disposition asymmetry that proves the shared-core claim  ← gap 0

The reference's §2 inventories both pawns' original components and derives the shared core from what is **and is not** shared (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:33-52`):

```
 PLAYER  TOG_Micheal_Character (7 BPI interfaces)      BOSS  BP_BaseEnemyAIV1_TOG (5 interfaces)
 ────────────────────────────────────────────────     ──────────────────────────────────────────
 AC_ComboSyst (combo buffer/window/reset) ── player    AC_EnemyAI (perception→blackboard) ── boss
 AC_SkillSystem (skills) ──────────────── player       BPC_Attacks_TOG (weighted pick) ──── boss
 AC_Parry/ParryAttackV2 (keystone) ─┐                   AC_ParryAttackV2 ──┐
 AC_Dodge (roll + i-frames) ── player │                 AC_Hitbox ─────────┤  SAME shared
 AC_LockOn (soft-target) ──── player  │ SHARED across   AC_HitReaction ────┘  abilities as player
 AC_QTE (vestigial) ───────── player  │ both pawns ↓
 AC_Hitbox (swing sweep) ───────────┤
 AC_HitReaction (reaction grid) ────┘
```

> **The boss has NO QTE/Dodge/LockOn/Skill/Combo components — those are player-only. This asymmetry confirms the shared core is *attack execution + hit reaction + parry*, granted to both; everything else is per-pawn ability sets** (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:52`).

This is the *evidence* for §3.2's "one shared core, two front-ends": the intersection of the two pawns' component sets is exactly `{AC_Hitbox, AC_HitReaction, AC_ParryAttackV2}` (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:50`), i.e. attack-execution + hit-reaction + parry — and that intersection is what GAS grants to both as shared abilities. The non-shared, player-only systems become **per-pawn ability sets**, not part of the core. Two of those player-only sets are confirmed-but-under-built: the **Skills system** (`AC_SkillSystem`, §3.16) and the unverified **Aim/ranged** path (sword throw / card, §3.17).

### Non-goals (explicitly out of scope, per code comments / reference)
- **Armor / resistance / block / crit math** — `UExecCalc_Damage` is a raw SetByCaller pass-through in this phase ("C1 scope: base damage pass-through only", `TOGDamageGameplayAbility.h:20`; "No attribute captures needed for the C1 pass-through", `ExecCalc_Damage.cpp:30`).
- **XP / leveling economy** — `IPlayerInterface` exists with default stubs; `ATOGPlayerState::Level` is a **replicated `UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_Level)` initialized to `1` (declared `TOGPlayerState.h:45-46`, `OnRep_Level` at :49, within the cited :44-49 block)**, "Phase D: hook to an XP / level-up system" — present and replicated for UI, but not driven by any leveling system yet.
- **QTE as a core system** — the reference reclassifies QTE as **vestigial**: every QTE montage slot in the data is empty/`None` (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:41,145`). Build only if a specific QTE moment is wanted.
- **Player heavies/specials beyond the in-use HEAVY chain** — the reference defers separate player specials; the in-use combo *is* the heavy chain, with specials routed through the Skills system when scoped (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:144`; §3.16).
- **AnimNext / Mover** — classic `ACharacter` + CMC + AnimBP only (`ONBOARDING.md`). `[ASSUMED]` CMC by UE default; no movement-component override exists in `Source`.
- **A 1:1 `AC_X → GAS_Y` port table** — explicitly rejected by the reference (`TOG-GAS-Architecture.md:5`).

### Designed but not-yet-implemented in C++ (design intent lives in the reference; see ledger §1.1)
Paired parry/counter/finisher execution (§3.7, §5.5) · the poise economy (§3.8, §5.6) · enemy AI (§3.9, §5.7) · anim-curve locomotion/turn (§3.6a) · the windowed hitbox sweep (§3.3, §3.6) · the AC_HitReaction AI-feedback loop (§3.6c) · the HUD/UI layer (§3.11) · weapon-swap/equipment (§3.12) · dodge/roll (§3.13) · lock-on as a real component (§3.14) · save/load (§3.15) · the **Skills** system (§3.16) · the **Aim/ranged** projectile path (§3.17, unverified scope) · the combo & air-combo systems (`FTOGComboRow`, no consumer; §5.8).
